message.content:
Below is a state‑by‑state list of **web‑app‑testable** requirements under the **Virginia VCDPA, Colorado CPA, Connecticut CTDPA, and Utah UCPA** (excluding California).  

I focus on requirements that can be evaluated from the front end or HTTP traffic (e.g., privacy notice content, rights request flows, consent UI, opt‑out links, recognition of a universal opt‑out mechanism). For each, I include:  

- **Check name**  
- **What it verifies (test description)**  
- **Which state(s)**  
- **Authoritative source + URL** (official statute / AG / legislature)

---

## 1. Scope / Applicability Disclosure (Optional Check for Mapping)

**Check name:** App discloses applicability or geographic scope (informational)

**What it verifies:**  
Whether the privacy notice or other public‑facing documentation indicates which state laws it is designed to comply with or which jurisdictions’ residents are covered (e.g., “This notice applies to residents of Virginia, Colorado, Connecticut, and Utah”). This is not a statutory requirement in these laws, but is often used to clarify scope and helps in test scoping.

**States:** All four (VCDPA, CPA, CTDPA, UCPA) – *not required but helpful for testing*.

**Authoritative sources (for scope definitions):**  
- **Virginia VCDPA:** Va. Code Ann. §§ 59.1‑575, 59.1‑576 (scope, definitions).[Va. Gen. Assembly LIS – Title 59.1, Ch. 52.1]  
  URL: `https://law.lis.virginia.gov/vacode/title59.1/chapter52.1/`  
- **Colorado CPA:** Colo. Rev. Stat. §§ 6‑1‑1303, 6‑1‑1304 (scope and applicability).[Colorado Revised Statutes – Title 6 Art. 1 Pt. 13]  
  URL: `https://leg.colorado.gov/sites/default/files/images/olls/crs2023-title-6.pdf` (CPA at §§ 6‑1‑1301 et seq.)  
- **Connecticut CTDPA:** Conn. Gen. Stat. §§ 42‑521, 42‑522.[Connecticut General Statutes – Title 42, Ch. 743dd]  
  URL: `https://www.cga.ct.gov/current/pub/chap_743dd.htm`  
- **Utah UCPA:** Utah Code §§ 13‑61‑101, 13‑61‑102.[Utah Code – Title 13, Ch. 61]  
  URL: `https://le.utah.gov/xcode/Title13/Chapter61/13-61.html`

---

## 2. Privacy Notice – Core Content Requirements

### 2.1. Basic Privacy Notice Presence

**Check name:** Privacy notice publicly available

**What it verifies:**  
The web app provides a **reasonably accessible, clear, and meaningful privacy notice** (usually via a link such as “Privacy Policy”) accessible without login.

**States & requirements:**  
- **Virginia VCDPA:** Controllers must provide “a reasonably accessible, clear, and meaningful privacy notice.”[Va. Code § 59.1‑578.A]  
- **Colorado CPA:** Controllers must provide a “reasonably accessible, clear, and meaningful privacy notice.”[Colo. Rev. Stat. § 6‑1‑1308(1)]  
- **Connecticut CTDPA:** Controllers must provide a “reasonably accessible, clear and meaningful privacy notice.”[Conn. Gen. Stat. § 42‑521(a)]  
- **Utah UCPA:** Controllers must “provide consumers with a reasonably accessible and clear privacy notice.”[Utah Code § 13‑61‑302(1)]

**Authoritative sources + URLs:**  
- VCDPA: Va. Code § 59.1‑578.[Virginia General Assembly – VCDPA]  
  `https://law.lis.virginia.gov/vacode/59.1-578/`  
- Colorado CPA: Colo. Rev. Stat. § 6‑1‑1308.[Colorado Revised Statutes]  
  `https://law.justia.com/codes/colorado/2023/title-6/article-1/part-13/section-6-1-1308/`  
- Connecticut CTDPA: Conn. Gen. Stat. § 42‑521.[CT General Statutes]  
  `https://www.cga.ct.gov/current/pub/chap_743dd.htm#sec_42-521`  
- Utah UCPA: Utah Code § 13‑61‑302.[Utah Code]  
  `https://le.utah.gov/xcode/Title13/Chapter61/13-61-S302.html`

---

### 2.2. Privacy Notice – Categories of Personal Data Processed

**Check name:** Notice lists categories of personal data processed

**What it verifies:**  
The privacy notice lists **categories of personal data** (e.g., identifiers, contact information, browsing data) processed about consumers.

**States:**  
- **Virginia, Colorado, Connecticut:** Explicitly required.  
- **Utah:** Required to “describe the categories of personal data processed” similarly.

**Legal requirements:**  
- VCDPA: Privacy notice must identify “the categories of personal data processed by the controller.”[Va. Code § 59.1‑578.A.1]  
- Colorado CPA: Notice must specify “the categories of personal data collected or processed by the controller.”[Colo. Rev. Stat. § 6‑1‑1308(1)(a)]  
- Connecticut CTDPA: Must include “the categories of personal data processed by the controller.”[Conn. Gen. Stat. § 42‑521(a)(1)]  
- Utah UCPA: Must describe “the categories of personal data processed by the controller.”[Utah Code § 13‑61‑302(1)(a)]

---

### 2.3. Privacy Notice – Purpose of Processing

**Check name:** Notice states purposes for processing personal data

**What it verifies:**  
The privacy notice explains **the purposes for which personal data is processed** (e.g., service provision, analytics, targeted advertising).

**States & requirements:**  
- VCDPA: Notice must identify “the purpose for processing personal data.”[Va. Code § 59.1‑578.A.2]  
- Colorado CPA: Notice must identify “the purposes for which the categories of personal data are processed.”[Colo. Rev. Stat. § 6‑1‑1308(1)(b)]  
- Connecticut CTDPA: Must describe “the purposes for which the categories of personal data are processed.”[Conn. Gen. Stat. § 42‑521(a)(2)]  
- Utah UCPA: Must describe “the purposes for which the categories of personal data are processed.”[Utah Code § 13‑61‑302(1)(b)]

---

### 2.4. Privacy Notice – Consumer Rights & How to Exercise Them

**Check name:** Notice describes consumer rights and how to exercise them

**What it verifies:**  
The notice lists consumer rights (access, deletion, correction, data portability, opt‑out, etc., as applicable by state) and **how** consumers can submit requests (e.g., webform, email, toll‑free number).

**States & requirements:**  
- VCDPA: Must “describe how consumers may exercise their consumer rights… and how a consumer may appeal a controller’s decision.”[Va. Code § 59.1‑578.A.3]  
- Colorado CPA: Must describe “how and where consumers may exercise the rights” including how to appeal.[Colo. Rev. Stat. § 6‑1‑1308(1)(c)]  
- Connecticut CTDPA: Must describe “how consumers may exercise their consumer rights… including how a consumer may appeal a controller’s decision.”[Conn. Gen. Stat. § 42‑521(a)(3)]  
- Utah UCPA: Must describe “how consumers may exercise the rights… provided by this chapter.”[Utah Code § 13‑61‑302(1)(c)] (Utah does not include correction and some other rights.)

**Web‑testable points:** look for explicit rights list, and at least one described submission method.

---

### 2.5. Privacy Notice – Categories of Personal Data Shared and With Whom

**Check name:** Notice lists categories of personal data shared and categories of third parties

**What it verifies:**  
The notice discloses **categories of personal data shared** and **categories of third parties** with whom data is shared.

**States & requirements:**  
- VCDPA: Must disclose “the categories of personal data that the controller shares with third parties, if any,” and “the categories of third parties, if any, with whom the controller shares personal data.”[Va. Code § 59.1‑578.A.4–5]  
- Colorado CPA: Must identify “the categories of third parties” with whom personal data is shared.[Colo. Rev. Stat. § 6‑1‑1308(1)(d)]  
- Connecticut CTDPA: Must identify “the categories of personal data that the controller shares with third parties, if any,” and “the categories of third parties, if any, with whom the controller shares personal data.”[Conn. Gen. Stat. § 42‑521(a)(4)–(5)]  
- Utah UCPA: Must describe “the categories of personal data that the controller shares with third parties, if any” and “the categories of third parties with whom the controller shares personal data, if any.”[Utah Code § 13‑61‑302(1)(d)–(e)]

---

### 2.6. Privacy Notice – Targeted Advertising / Sale / Profiling Opt‑Out Disclosure

**Check name:** Notice discloses targeted advertising / sale / profiling and right to opt out

**What it verifies:**  
If the controller engages in **sale of personal data**, **targeted advertising**, or **profiling in furtherance of decisions that produce legal or similarly significant effects**, the notice discloses that activity and informs consumers of their **right to opt out**.

**States & requirements:**  
- VCDPA: Notice must disclose if data is sold or used for targeted advertising and “how a consumer may exercise the right to opt out.”[Va. Code § 59.1‑578.A.5; § 59.1‑577.A.4–5]  
- Colorado CPA: Notice must clearly disclose “if a controller sells personal data to, or shares it with, third parties, and, if so, the categories of personal data sold or shared,” and the “means” for exercising the right to opt out of sale, targeted advertising, and profiling.[Colo. Rev. Stat. §§ 6‑1‑1306(1)(a)(III), (b); 6‑1‑1308(1)(e)]  
- Connecticut CTDPA: Similar disclosure requirement if the controller sells personal data or processes it for targeted advertising, plus description of the opt‑out mechanism.[Conn. Gen. Stat. §§ 42‑518(a)(5); 42‑521(a)(6)]  
- Utah UCPA: If the controller sells personal data or engages in targeted advertising, the controller must “clearly and conspicuously disclose” that and how a consumer may opt out.[Utah Code § 13‑61‑302(2); § 13‑61‑301(1)(a)]

**Web‑testable points:** explicit mention of sale/targeted advertising/profiling and opt‑out options.

---

### 2.7. Privacy Notice – Processing of Sensitive Data

**Check name:** Notice discloses processing of sensitive data and indicates required consent/opt‑out model

**What it verifies:**  
Whether the notice discloses processing of **sensitive data** and, where required, indicates that such processing occurs only with **opt‑in consent** (or opt‑out in Utah).

**States & legal standard:**  
- **Virginia VCDPA (opt‑in):** Controller “shall not process sensitive data concerning a consumer without obtaining the consumer’s consent.”[Va. Code § 59.1‑578.A.6; § 59.1‑575]  
- **Colorado CPA (opt‑in):** Controller must “not process sensitive data concerning a consumer without first obtaining the consumer’s consent.”[Colo. Rev. Stat. § 6‑1‑1308(7); § 6‑1‑1303(24)]  
- **Connecticut CTDPA (opt‑in):** Controller shall not process sensitive data without the consumer’s consent.[Conn. Gen. Stat. § 42‑520(b)(4); § 42‑519(27)]  
- **Utah UCPA (opt‑out):** Controller “may not process a consumer’s sensitive data” without first providing **clear notice** and giving consumers an **opportunity to opt out** of such processing.[Utah Code § 13‑61‑302(3); § 13‑61‑101(32)]

**Web‑testable points:**  
- Existence of a sensitive‑data disclosure.  
- Opt‑in UI for VA/CO/CT (e.g., unchecked checkbox) vs notice + opt‑out for Utah.

---

## 3. Consumer Rights Request Mechanisms

### 3.1. Rights Request Mechanism – Presence and Methods

**Check name:** Web‑accessible method to submit consumer rights requests

**What it verifies:**  
The web app provides at least one **conspicuous and user‑friendly method** to submit requests to exercise rights (access, deletion, correction, etc.), such as a form, portal, email link, or account interface.

**States & requirements:**  
- **Rights themselves** (high‑level):  
  - VCDPA: rights to access, correct, delete, data portability, and opt‑out.[Va. Code § 59.1‑577]  
  - Colorado CPA: similar rights.[Colo. Rev. Stat. § 6‑1‑1306(1)(a)]  
  - Connecticut CTDPA: similar rights.[Conn. Gen. Stat. § 42‑518(a)]  
  - Utah UCPA: rights to access, delete, and data portability, plus opt‑out of sale and targeted advertising (no correction right).[Utah Code § 13‑61‑201]

- **Mechanism requirement:**  
  - VCDPA: Controller must establish “one or more secure and reliable means for consumers to submit a request” that is described in the notice.[Va. Code § 59.1‑578.A.3; § 59.1‑577.B]  
  - Colorado CPA: Controller must provide “a secure and reliable means for consumers to submit a request to exercise their rights,” taking into account how consumers normally interact.[Colo. Rev. Stat. § 6‑1‑1306(1)(b)]  
  - Connecticut CTDPA: Very similar “secure and reliable means” requirement.[Conn. Gen. Stat. § 42‑518(b)]  
  - Utah UCPA: Requires a method for consumers to submit a request; controller must respond within statutory timelines.[Utah Code § 13‑61‑203(1)–(4)]

**Web‑testable:** presence of at least one channel; ideally a form or link labeled “privacy rights,” “data rights,” “Do Not Sell,” etc.

---

### 3.2. Appeal Mechanism for Denied Requests

**Check name:** Web‑accessible appeal mechanism information (where required)

**What it verifies:**  
When a request is denied, the controller provides a **method to appeal** and clearly discloses how to do so.

**States & requirements:**  
- **Virginia VCDPA:** Controller must “establish a process for a consumer to appeal the controller’s refusal to take action on a request” and describe that process in the privacy notice.[Va. Code § 59.1‑577.E; § 59.1‑578.A.3]  
- **Colorado CPA:** Controller must provide an internal appeal process and describe it in the privacy policy.[Colo. Rev. Stat. § 6‑1‑1306(3)(a); § 6‑1‑1308(1)(c)]  
- **Connecticut CTDPA:** Same concept – must establish an appeal process and describe it.[Conn. Gen. Stat. § 42‑518(e); § 42‑521(a)(3)]  
- **Utah UCPA:** No statutory requirement for an appeal mechanism.

**Web‑testable:**  
Look for appeal instructions in the privacy notice and/or in rights request UI (e.g., “If we deny your request, you may appeal by emailing…”).

---

## 4. Opt‑Out of Targeted Advertising, Sale, Profiling

### 4.1. Opt‑Out Links / Mechanisms

**Check name:** Opt‑out mechanism for targeted advertising and sale (and profiling where required)

**What it verifies:**  
Whether the web app provides a **clear, conspicuous method** to opt out of:  
- targeted advertising  
- sale of personal data  
- profiling in furtherance of decisions that produce legal or similarly significant effects (where applicable).

**States & requirements (high‑level):**

- **Virginia VCDPA:**  
  - Right to opt out of targeted advertising, sale, and profiling.[Va. Code § 59.1‑577.A.4–6]  
  - Must provide a means for consumers to exercise these rights.[Va. Code § 59.1‑577.B; § 59.1‑578.A.3]

- **Colorado CPA:**  
  - Right to opt out of sale, targeted advertising, and certain profiling.[Colo. Rev. Stat. § 6‑1‑1306(1)(a)(III)]  
  - Must clearly describe and offer a means to exercise this right, including through a **universal opt‑out mechanism** (see § 4.2 below).[Colo. Rev. Stat. §§ 6‑1‑1306(1)(b); 6‑1‑1306(1)(a)(III); 6‑1‑1313]

- **Connecticut CTDPA:**  
  - Right to opt out of targeted advertising, sale, and certain profiling.[Conn. Gen. Stat. § 42‑518(a)(5)]  
  - Controller must provide mechanisms for exercising these rights.[Conn. Gen. Stat. § 42‑518(b)]

- **Utah UCPA:**  
  - Right to opt out of sale and targeted advertising only (no profiling right).[Utah Code § 13‑61‑203(2)]  
  - Controller must provide a clear and conspicuous method to opt out when selling or engaging in targeted advertising (often via a link or settings).[Utah Code § 13‑61‑302(2)]

**Web‑testable:**  
Look for “Do Not Sell or Share,” “Ad Choices,” “Your Privacy Choices,” or similar links; confirm they lead to a workable opt‑out function.

---

### 4.2. Universal Opt‑Out Mechanism (e.g., Global Privacy Control)

**Key point you requested:**  

- **Colorado CPA and Connecticut CTDPA REQUIRE honoring a universal opt‑out mechanism.**  
- **Virginia VCDPA and Utah UCPA do NOT require universal opt‑out mechanism recognition.**

**Check name:** Universal Opt‑Out Mechanism (UOOM) recognition

**What it verifies:**  
Whether the web app (client‑side and/or server‑side) **detects and honors a user’s opt‑out preference signal** sent via a universal mechanism (e.g., Global Privacy Control (GPC)), at least for Colorado and Connecticut users.

**States & requirements:**

- **Colorado CPA – Required**

  - The CPA directs the AG to establish rules for a **“universal opt‑out mechanism”** and requires controllers to “allow consumers to exercise the right to opt out… through a user‑selected universal opt‑out mechanism.”[Colo. Rev. Stat. § 6‑1‑1313(2)]  
  - Controllers must recognize and honor a mechanism that the Colorado AG has approved and published.[Colo. Rev. Stat. § 6‑1‑1313(2)(a)–(b)]  
  - Colorado Privacy Act Rules (AG) provide detail that controllers must treat a valid UOOM signal as an opt‑out of targeted advertising and sale.

  **Authoritative source:**  
  - Colo. Rev. Stat. § 6‑1‑1313.[Colorado Revised Statutes – CPA]  
    `https://law.justia.com/codes/colorado/2023/title-6/article-1/part-13/section-6-1-1313/`  
  - Colorado Privacy Act Rules – 4 CCR 904‑3 (AG website).[Colorado AG]

- **Connecticut CTDPA – Required**

  - CTDPA requires the AG to adopt regulations specifying one or more **opt‑out preference signals** and requires controllers to comply with them.[Conn. Gen. Stat. § 42‑522(b)(1)–(4)]  
  - As of July 1, 2025, controllers must “allow consumers to opt out of any processing for the purposes of targeted advertising, sale, or profiling… through an opt‑out preference signal.”[Conn. Gen. Stat. § 42‑522(b)]  

  **Authoritative source:**  
  - Conn. Gen. Stat. § 42‑522(b).[Connecticut General Statutes – CTDPA]  
    `https://www.cga.ct.gov/current/pub/chap_743dd.htm#sec_42-522`  

- **Virginia VCDPA – Not required**

  - VCDPA provides opt‑out rights but does **not** mandate recognition of a universal opt‑out mechanism in the statute.[Va. Code §§ 59.1‑577, 59.1‑578]  

  **Authoritative source:** Va. Code Ann. Ch. 52.1 (no UOOM provision).[Virginia General Assembly – VCDPA]

- **Utah UCPA – Not required**

  - UCPA provides an opt‑out right but has **no statutory requirement** to honor a universal opt‑out preference signal.[Utah Code §§ 13‑61‑201 to 203; 13‑61‑302]  

  **Authoritative source:** Utah Code Title 13, Ch. 61 (no UOOM section).[Utah Legislature]

**Web‑testable:** send a GPC / similar signal and verify whether cookies, tracking, and ad‑related processing are disabled and/or a server‑side flag is set, specifically for CO and CT flows.

---

## 5. Sensitive Data Consent / Opt‑Out UI

### 5.1. Opt‑In Consent for Sensitive Data (VA / CO / CT)

**Check name:** Explicit opt‑in consent UI for sensitive data (Virginia, Colorado, Connecticut)

**What it verifies:**  
For **Virginia, Colorado, and Connecticut residents**, any processing of **sensitive data** is gated by a **clear, affirmative, freely given opt‑in consent** mechanism (e.g., unticked checkbox, “I agree” toggle), and there is no pre‑ticked box or bundled consent.

**Statutory requirements (and your requested emphasis):**

- **Virginia VCDPA:**  
  - Sensitive data definition includes racial or ethnic origin, religious beliefs, health diagnosis, sexual orientation, citizenship or immigration status; genetic or biometric data; precise geolocation; and children’s data.[Va. Code § 59.1‑575]  
  - “A controller shall not process sensitive data concerning a consumer without obtaining the consumer’s consent.”[Va. Code § 59.1‑578.A.6]  

- **Colorado CPA:**  
  - Similar definition of sensitive data.[Colo. Rev. Stat. § 6‑1‑1303(24)]  
  - Controller “shall not process a consumer’s sensitive data without first obtaining the consumer’s consent.”[Colo. Rev. Stat. § 6‑1‑1308(7)]  

- **Connecticut CTDPA:**  
  - Sensitive data defined in § 42‑519(27).[Conn. Gen. Stat. § 42‑519(27)]  
  - Controller shall “not process sensitive data concerning a consumer without obtaining the consumer’s consent.”[Conn. Gen. Stat. § 42‑520(b)(4)]  

**Web‑testable:** presence of explicit consent prompts before collecting e.g. precise location, health, biometric data, etc., for VA/CO/CT flows, and ability to withdraw consent.

---

### 5.2. Opt‑Out Model for Sensitive Data (Utah)

**Check name:** Notice + opt‑out mechanism for sensitive data (Utah)

**What it verifies:**  
If the controller processes sensitive data of Utah consumers, the site:  
- provides **clear notice** of that sensitive data processing; and  
- provides an **opportunity to opt out** (e.g., setting, toggle, or link).

**Statutory requirement (your requested emphasis):**

- **Utah UCPA – opt‑out, not opt‑in:**  
  - Utah defines “sensitive data” similarly (racial/ethnic origin, religious beliefs, sexual orientation, citizenship/immigration, medical history, etc.).[Utah Code § 13‑61‑101(32)]  
  - A controller “may not process a consumer’s sensitive data” without first providing the consumer **with clear notice and an opportunity to opt out** of the processing.[Utah Code § 13‑61‑302(3)]

**Web‑testable:** a notice and opt‑out control for Utah‑resident flows, rather than an opt‑in gate.

---

## 6. Data Protection Assessments (DPIAs) – Disclosure in Notice

These assessments are **not themselves web‑testable**, but the requirement to perform them can be cross‑checked against what the notice states about high‑risk processing.

**Check name (optional informational):** High‑risk processing disclosures aligned with DPIA triggers

**What it verifies:**  
Whether the site clearly indicates when it conducts **targeted advertising, sale of personal data, profiling, or sensitive data processing** that would require data protection assessments under state law. (The DPIA itself is internal.)

**States & legal triggers:**  
- **Virginia VCDPA:** DPIAs required for processing activities that present a “heightened risk of harm,” including targeted advertising, sale of personal data, processing sensitive data, and certain profiling.[Va. Code § 59.1‑579.A]  
- **Colorado CPA:** Impact assessments required for processing that presents a “heightened risk of harm,” including targeted advertising, sale, sensitive data, and profiling.[Colo. Rev. Stat. § 6‑1‑1309(1)]  
- **Connecticut CTDPA:** Similar requirement for data protection assessments for high‑risk processing.[Conn. Gen. Stat. § 42‑521(b)]  
- **Utah UCPA:** No general DPIA requirement.

**Authoritative sources:**  
- VCDPA: Va. Code § 59.1‑579.[Virginia General Assembly]  
  `https://law.lis.virginia.gov/vacode/59.1-579/`  
- Colorado CPA: Colo. Rev. Stat. § 6‑1‑1309.[Colorado Revised Statutes]  
- Connecticut CTDPA: Conn. Gen. Stat. § 42‑521(b).[Connecticut General Statutes]

---

## 7. Non‑Discrimination / No Retaliation for Exercising Rights

**Check name:** Notice states no discrimination for exercising privacy rights

**What it verifies:**  
Whether the notice states that the controller will **not discriminate against consumers** (e.g., by denying goods/services or charging different prices) for exercising their rights, except as allowed (e.g., bona fide loyalty programs).

**States & requirements:**  
- **Virginia VCDPA:** Controllers may not discriminate against a consumer for exercising rights, with limited exceptions.[Va. Code § 59.1‑578.D]  
- **Colorado CPA:** Controller “shall not unlawfully discriminate” against a consumer for exercising rights (e.g., deny goods, charge different price) but may offer loyalty programs under conditions.[Colo. Rev. Stat. § 6‑1‑1308(4)–(5)]  
- **Connecticut CTDPA:** Similar non‑discrimination requirement with allowed loyalty programs.[Conn. Gen. Stat. § 42‑521(c)]  
- **Utah UCPA:** Prohibits discrimination for exercising rights, with similar caveats.[Utah Code § 13‑61‑301(2)]

**Web‑testable:** Notice language and any visible pricing/benefit differences explicitly tied to rights exercise.

---

## 8. Children’s Data – Age‑Based Consent / Protections

These laws intersect with COPPA but add some **state‑level obligations**:

**Check name:** Enhanced protections / consent model for teens (13–17) where applicable

**What it verifies:**  
Whether the app’s flows for teens (13–17) reflect the state‑specific rules around targeted advertising, sale, and profiling requiring **opt‑in consent** or restrictions.

**States & requirements (high‑level):**  
- **Virginia VCDPA:** For consumers aged 13–17, processing for targeted advertising, sale, or certain profiling requires **opt‑in consent**.[Va. Code § 59.1‑577.A.4–6; interpreting “known child” alongside COPPA]  
- **Colorado CPA:** Requires **opt‑in consent** for processing children’s personal data for targeted advertising or sale for consumers ages 13–15 with actual knowledge (as detailed in AG rules).[Colo. Rev. Stat. § 6‑1‑1308(7)(b)]  
- **Connecticut CTDPA:** If the controller has actual knowledge that the consumer is 13–16, it must obtain the consumer’s consent for targeted advertising or sale.[Conn. Gen. Stat. § 42‑520(b)(5)]  
- **Utah UCPA:** Relies largely on COPPA; no independent teen‑opt‑in rule on targeted advertising/sale like CT or CO, though general protections apply.

**Authoritative sources:**  
- Connecticut CTDPA: teen consent – Conn. Gen. Stat. § 42‑520(b)(5).[Connecticut General Statutes]  
- Colorado CPA: children’s sensitive data and consent – Colo. Rev. Stat. § 6‑1‑1308(7)(b).[Colorado Revised Statutes]  
- Virginia VCDPA: rights and protections; children’s data implicitly covered as sensitive data and via COPPA reference.[Va. Code § 59.1‑575; § 59.1‑577.A.6]

**Web‑testable:**  
- Age gates or self‑declared age flows.  
- Additional consent prompts for 13–15/16 in CO and CT before targeted advertising or sale.

---

## 9. Security & Data Minimization (Disclosure Aspect)

The substantive security controls are not web‑testable, but some **disclosures** are.

**Check name (informational):** Notice describes security and retention/minimization practices

**What it verifies:**  
Privacy notice includes at least high‑level information consistent with statutory **security** and **data minimization** requirements (e.g., only collecting what is “adequate, relevant, and reasonably necessary” and using reasonable security measures).

**States & legal duties:**  
- VCDPA: data minimization; processing limited to what is “adequate, relevant, and reasonably necessary,” and reasonable security practices.[Va. Code § 59.1‑578.B–C]  
- Colorado CPA: similar data minimization and security duty.[Colo. Rev. Stat. § 6‑1‑1307(1)(a)–(c)]  
- Connecticut CTDPA: similar obligations.[Conn. Gen. Stat. § 42‑520(a)]  
- Utah UCPA: duty to maintain reasonable security practices, though no explicit minimization language.[Utah Code § 13‑61‑301(1)(b)]

**Authoritative sources:**  
- VCDPA: Va. Code § 59.1‑578.B–C.  
- Colorado CPA: Colo. Rev. Stat. § 6‑1‑1307(1).  
- CTDPA: Conn. Gen. Stat. § 42‑520(a).  
- UCPA: Utah Code § 13‑61‑301(1)(b).

**Web‑testable:** existence of statements about “reasonable security” and limited data collection, though you can’t verify the controls themselves from the web app.

---

## 10. Contracting with Processors – Public Facing Indicators

The actual contracts are not web‑visible, but sometimes a site discloses processor categories.

**Check name (informational):** Notice identifies use of processors / service providers

**What it verifies:**  
The privacy notice identifies categories of **processors/service providers** and distinguishes them from “third parties,” aligning with statutory controller‑processor requirements.

**States & requirements:**  
- All four statutes require specific controller‑processor contracts with obligations on processors.[Va. Code § 59.1‑579.B; Colo. Rev. Stat. § 6‑1‑1305; Conn. Gen. Stat. § 42‑521(c); Utah Code § 13‑61‑301(3)]  

**Web‑testable:** limited – look for language such as “service providers,” “processors,” etc., mapping to these categories.

---

## 11. Summary of Key Distinctions You Highlighted

To center on the two distinctions you asked to “note especially”:

| Issue | Virginia VCDPA | Colorado CPA | Connecticut CTDPA | Utah UCPA |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Universal Opt‑Out Mechanism (e.g., GPC)** | **Not required** – no UOOM provision in statute.[Va. Code §§ 59.1‑577–578] | **Required** – must recognize AG‑designated UOOM for opt‑out of sale & targeted advertising.[Colo. Rev. Stat. § 6‑1‑1313] | **Required** – must allow opt‑out via opt‑out preference signal per AG regulations.[Conn. Gen. Stat. § 42‑522(b)] | **Not required** – statute has no UOOM obligation.[Utah Code Title 13, Ch. 61] |
| **Sensitive Data Legal Standard** | **Opt‑in consent required** before processing sensitive data.[Va. Code § 59.1‑578.A.6] | **Opt‑in consent required** before processing sensitive data.[Colo. Rev. Stat. § 6‑1‑1308(7)] | **Opt‑in consent required** before processing sensitive data.[Conn. Gen. Stat. § 42‑520(b)(4)] | **Opt‑out model** – must give clear notice and an opportunity to opt out before processing sensitive data.[Utah Code § 13‑61‑302(3)] |
